event: Abishai saves David's life from Ishbi-Benob the Philistine giant
symbols: stormy sea and waves — the chaos of battle and mortal danger threatening God's anointed
sword and spear — instruments of both threat and deliverance in holy warfare
halo on David — divine election and anointing of God's chosen king
sinking figures — the overwhelming peril from which only divine intervention through loyal companions can rescue
birds in dark sky — scavengers of war, ominous portents of death
description: A dramatic black-and-white engraving depicts a violent scene at sea or in turbulent waters. In the foreground, a figure with a halo (David) clings to the edge of a boat or wreckage amid crashing waves. Another warrior (Abishai) fights nearby, weapons raised, defending the endangered king. Multiple armored figures struggle in the churning water, some falling or already overcome. The sky is pitch dark with birds circling overhead. The composition conveys extreme peril, desperate combat, and the chaos of battle.
theological_description: The episode of Abishai saving David's life is recorded in 2 Samuel 21:15-17, during one of the later wars between Israel and the Philistines. David, grown weary in battle, was in mortal danger from Ishbi-Benob, a descendant of the giants (Rephaim), who carried a new sword and intended to kill the king. Abishai son of Zeruiah came to David's aid and struck the Philistine down. After this, David's men swore an oath that he would no longer go out to battle, 'so that you do not quench the lamp of Israel.' This phrase is theologically charged: David is the 'lamp' (ner) of Israel, the anointed vessel through whose lineage the Messiah will come (2 Samuel 7:12-16). To extinguish David is to threaten the entire messianic promise. Abishai's rescue therefore has cosmic significance -- he preserves the Davidic line through which salvation history will proceed to Christ. The loyalty of the mighty men reflects the covenantal faithfulness (hesed) that binds Israel together, a human echo of God's own faithful protection of his anointed. The scene also prefigures the pattern seen throughout the Psalms where David cries out from mortal danger and God delivers him (Psalm 18:4-6, 16-19), often through human instruments. The turbulent waters evoke the chaotic waters of death from which God rescues the psalmist (Psalm 69:1-2, 14-15).

scripture_refs.reason: The direct narrative: David grows weary fighting the Philistines, Ishbi-Benob threatens to kill him, and Abishai intervenes to save his life.
The Davidic covenant -- God's promise of an eternal dynasty -- is at stake in this rescue, giving it messianic significance.
The cords of death encompassed me; I called to the Lord -- David's pattern of crying out in mortal peril and being delivered.
He drew me out of many waters; he rescued me from my strong enemy -- the waters of death imagery matching the scene.
Save me, O God, for the waters have come up to my neck -- the engulfing waters of mortal danger.
The catalog of David's mighty men and their heroic exploits, the broader context of Abishai's loyalty.
